speaker: doctor
text: Good morning. I see you're currently on Lisinopril 20mg once daily for your blood pressure control.

speaker: patient
text: Yes, but lately I've been feeling quite dizzy—especially when standing up quickly.

speaker: doctor
text: I just reviewed your point-of-care blood glucose—it's elevated at 14.3 mmol/L. That’s significantly above target.

speaker: patient
text: Really? I haven’t been monitoring it regularly—I thought the dizziness was just from the blood pressure meds.

speaker: doctor
text: Your diastolic blood pressure today is 79 mmHg—within normal range—but let’s hold that thought while we look at the full picture.

speaker: patient
text: I’ve also felt unusually tired and short of breath walking up stairs.

speaker: doctor
text: That’s important to note. Given your persistent hypertension and symptoms, we’ll need to reassess your regimen carefully.

speaker: doctor
text: Based on your history and today’s findings, I’m confirming a diagnosis of Essential Hypertension.

speaker: doctor
text: Your hemoglobin is low at 98 g/L—suggesting mild anemia, which could contribute to fatigue and dizziness.

speaker: doctor
text: Your systolic pressure is elevated at 172 mmHg—so despite Lisinopril, your BP remains uncontrolled.

speaker: patient
text: Could the dizziness be related to the anemia—or maybe my blood sugar?

speaker: doctor
text: Given the inadequate BP control and side effects, I am going to switch you to Amlodipine 5mg once daily instead of Lisinopril.

speaker: patient
text: Okay—I’ll start that tomorrow. Should I still check my blood sugar?

speaker: doctor
text: Yes, absolutely. Let’s monitor it closely—especially since hyperglycemia can worsen both dizziness and fatigue.

speaker: patient
text: I did check once this morning before coming in—my fingerstick reading was high again.

speaker: doctor
text: We’ll need to investigate possible undiagnosed diabetes or medication-related hyperglycemia.

speaker: doctor
text: Actually, your repeat glucose reading just now is even higher—16.0 mmol/L—so this confirms ongoing significant hyperglycemia.

speaker: patient
text: That’s concerning. Could the anemia be getting worse too? I’ve felt more pale and weak lately.

speaker: doctor
text: Unfortunately, yes—the latest hemoglobin is now down to 80 g/L, indicating progression of anemia that needs urgent evaluation.

speaker: patient
text: Wait—I remember your earlier note said it was 98 g/L. Is that the same number you mentioned before?
